telos: A web-based daily dashboard that, when opened in a browser, shows current Major League Baseball team standings, weekly performance trends, and upcoming game schedules for all teams.
restatement: Build a static web page that displays a daily-updated snapshot of all 30 MLB teams: overall standings/rankings, weekly performance trends (last-7-days W-L and run differential), and upcoming game schedules. The page is hosted on GitHub Pages (static hosting) and fetches live MLB data client-side when a visitor loads it.

proper_nouns.lexical_context: Daily dashboard for all mlb teams and statistics
proper_nouns.notes: Major League Baseball — the professional North American baseball league with 30 teams (AL + NL, 6 divisions). Data subject of the build. Canonical public data source is the MLB Stats API at https://statsapi.mlb.com/api/v1/.

GitHub's static-site hosting product. Constrains the deliverable to static assets (HTML/CSS/JS + JSON), no server-side runtime. The auto-builder repo already has Pages enabled at mondrianaire.github.io/auto-builder; this build deploys under runs/mlb-daily-dashboard/output/final/.

assumption_ledger.assumption: The deliverable is a single web page (dashboard) loaded in a desktop browser by visiting a URL.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Prompt says 'web based ... dashboard' and 'Use github pages for hosting'.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If the user wanted a native or installable app, the deliverable would not satisfy first-contact.

assumption_ledger.assumption: The dashboard covers all 30 current MLB teams.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: Filtering to a subset (e.g., only AL or favorited teams) would miss stated scope.

assumption_ledger.assumption: The dashboard presents three primary content blocks: (a) weekly trends, (b) upcoming games and schedules, (c) overall rankings/standings.
assumption_ledger.rationale: These three are the explicit focus of the prompt's 'focusing on ...' clause.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: Missing any of the three would leave a stated focus area unrepresented.

assumption_ledger.assumption: Statistics are team-level (W-L record, win pct, runs scored/allowed, run differential, streak) rather than player-level.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Prompt frames the dashboard around teams and overall rankings; team-level stats are the simpler/canonical dashboard granularity. Player-level would expand scope dramatically.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If user expected player leaderboards (HR leaders, ERA leaders), they would feel the dashboard is shallow.

assumption_ledger.assumption: 'Daily' means the data the user sees reflects the current day each time the page is loaded — achieved via client-side fetch on page load, since GitHub Pages is static hosting.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Static hosting cannot push updates. The simplest way to give a visitor today's data is to fetch from a public MLB feed on load. Pre-baked JSON via CI is an alternative but adds infrastructure.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If user expected an emailed/pushed daily digest, the in-browser model misses intent.
assumption_ledger.display_label: Live fetch on each page load

assumption_ledger.assumption: 'Weekly trends' = each team's record and run differential over the last 7 calendar days, displayed as a per-team mini-trend (W-L line plus run differential).
assumption_ledger.rationale: Simplest defensible interpretation of 'weekly trends' for a team dashboard. Run differential + W-L is the canonical 'how is this team trending' pair.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: User might have meant calendar-week (Mon-Sun) windows or longer momentum metrics.

assumption_ledger.assumption: 'Upcoming games and schedules' = today's slate plus the next ~7 days of scheduled games for each team.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Matches the 'daily' framing while giving enough lookahead to be useful for someone scanning the week.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: Could be too short (user wanted full month) or too long (user wanted only today).

assumption_ledger.assumption: 'Overall rankings' = MLB-wide standings broken out by division (6 divisions), with each team's W-L and games-back; wild-card race optional.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Division standings are the canonical 'overall rankings' shape in MLB. League-wide flat ranking is uncommon.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: User might have meant a flat 1-30 power ranking instead.

assumption_ledger.assumption: Read-only, no auth, no user accounts, no preferences persisted across sessions.
assumption_ledger.rationale: Prompt requests a dashboard, not a personalization product. Static hosting constraint reinforces this.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: User would not be able to favorite teams or save settings; acceptable for v1.

assumption_ledger.assumption: Host OS for the build process is Windows; the deliverable itself is OS-agnostic (runs in any modern browser).
assumption_ledger.rationale: Execution-context evidence shows Windows paths (Principle E).
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: Any toolchain that assumed POSIX-only (e.g., shell scripts in build pipeline) would fail on the host.

assumption_ledger.assumption: The 2026 MLB regular season is in progress on the build date (2026-05-26); the API will return real games and standings.
assumption_ledger.rationale: MLB regular season runs late-March through late-September; May 26 is mid-season.
assumption_ledger.what_breaks_if_wrong: If for any reason there are no games today (off-day), the page must show a clean empty state rather than break.

inflection_points.topic: Data source for MLB schedule, standings, and team statistics
choices: MLB Stats API (statsapi.mlb.com) — official public JSON feed used by the MLB.com site itself
Third-party scraped/derived sources (ESPN unofficial endpoints, sports-data aggregators)
inflection_points.importance_action_evidence: statsapi.mlb.com is the canonical, CORS-accessible, no-key-required JSON feed maintained by MLB Advanced Media. It is the documented data source behind MLB.com's own pages and is well-documented in the open-source community (e.g., toddrob99/MLB-StatsAPI Python wrapper, multiple JS clients). For a static GitHub-Pages dashboard fetching client-side, this is the only source that satisfies (a) free, (b) no API key embedded in static assets, (c) CORS-permissive, (d) authoritative. TD should confirm the specific endpoints (/schedule, /standings, /teams/{id}/stats) and CORS behavior; if CORS fails from github.io origin, a pre-baked JSON snapshot built by a GitHub Action becomes the fallback.
inflection_points.why_inflection: Without a chosen data source the build cannot begin; the source choice cascades into refresh strategy, schema shape, and error handling. The wrong choice (e.g., scraper that breaks on HTML changes) makes the dashboard fragile.

inflection_points.topic: How 'daily' freshness is delivered on a static host
choices: Client-side fetch on every page load + optional in-page interval refresh
GitHub Actions cron job that rebuilds a JSON snapshot daily and commits it; page reads the snapshot
Hybrid: snapshot for initial paint, live fetch in background
inflection_points.default_branch: Client-side fetch on every page load
inflection_points.importance_action_evidence: Client-side fetch is the simplest path that genuinely gives each visitor today's data, requires zero CI infrastructure, and matches the static-hosting constraint. If TD discovers CORS blocks the MLB Stats API from a github.io origin, the fallback is the GitHub Actions cron build — TD should validate CORS as part of contract verification before final commit.
inflection_points.why_inflection: GitHub Pages cannot run server code; 'daily' must be realized either at request-time (browser) or build-time (CI). The two paths produce very different repository shapes.

choices: Team-level only (W-L, run diff, ERA, batting avg as team)
Team-level + selected player leaderboards (HR, AVG, ERA, WHIP)
Full player drill-down per team
inflection_points.importance_action_evidence: Prompt repeatedly frames the dashboard around teams ('all mlb teams', 'overall rankings'). Team-level is the simplest defensible reading. Adding player drill-down materially expands scope and UI complexity.
inflection_points.why_inflection: Granularity choice has the largest single impact on UI density and data-volume per page.

choices: Rolling last-7-days W-L + run differential delta per team
Calendar-week (Mon-Sun) W-L per team
Last 10 games (a common MLB convention) instead of 7
inflection_points.default_branch: Rolling last-7-days W-L + run differential delta per team
inflection_points.importance_action_evidence: 'Weekly' literally maps to a 7-day window. Rolling-7 is more meaningful for a daily dashboard than calendar-week (which would be empty on a Monday morning).
inflection_points.why_inflection: Picks the time window that defines what 'trend' means in the UI.

choices: Today only
Today + next 7 days
Full remainder of season
inflection_points.importance_action_evidence: 'Daily dashboard' + 'upcoming games and schedules' suggests near-term horizon. 7 days matches the weekly framing already chosen and keeps the page scannable.
inflection_points.why_inflection: Lookahead window drives schedule table size and visual layout.

choices: Six division standings tables (AL E/C/W, NL E/C/W) + wild-card race
Flat 1-through-30 league-wide ranking by win pct
Both
inflection_points.default_branch: Six division standings tables + wild-card race
inflection_points.importance_action_evidence: Division standings are the canonical MLB ranking presentation; wild-card race is what fans check for postseason positioning. A flat 1-30 is uncommon in MLB context.
inflection_points.why_inflection: Determines the structural backbone of the rankings section.

inflection_points.topic: Behavior on off-days or when API returns no games
choices: Show empty-state copy ('No games scheduled today')
Show yesterday's results instead
Hide the section
inflection_points.importance_action_evidence: Empty state preserves UI layout and is honest; silently swapping in yesterday's data would mislead.
inflection_points.why_inflection: Edge case but inevitable — MLB has scheduled off-days even mid-season.

choices: Team abbreviations + official primary color swatches (derivable from MLB Stats API)
Team logos as images (requires hotlinking from MLB CDN or bundling assets)
Text-only, no color
inflection_points.importance_action_evidence: Color swatches are visually meaningful and avoid the licensing/hotlinking risks of bundling official logos. Abbreviations are unambiguous for a baseball-literate audience.
inflection_points.why_inflection: Touches IP/licensing if logos are bundled; defaulting to text+color is the safe path.

first_contact_requirements.description: User visits the live URL in a desktop browser; the page loads successfully (HTTP 200, no blank screen, no console-fatal errors).
Within a few seconds of load, the dashboard renders actual current MLB data — not placeholder text, not 'Loading...' indefinitely, not an error message.
All three focus areas are visibly present on the page: (a) weekly trends per team, (b) upcoming games / schedules, (c) overall rankings / standings.
All 30 MLB teams are represented somewhere on the page (in standings, in trends, or in the schedule).
The data shown is recognizably current (e.g., standings reflect a date within the last day or two, schedule shows games dated today or in the near future).
The page is hosted at a publicly reachable URL on GitHub Pages and the URL is delivered to the user.
On a same-day or next-day return visit, the displayed data has refreshed to reflect the new day's standings and schedule.

out_of_scope: Gambling, betting odds, or sportsbook integration
Fantasy baseball drafting, lineup-setting, or league management
Ticket purchasing or links to ticket vendors
Push notifications, email digests, or any out-of-browser delivery
User accounts, authentication, or saved preferences (favorite team, etc.)
Player-level statistics, leaderboards, or player drill-down pages
Live in-game pitch-by-pitch or play-by-play feeds
Historical season archives or year-over-year comparisons
Postseason bracket simulators or playoff probability models
Native mobile app or installable PWA shell
Server-side backend, database, or any non-static infrastructure
Bundled official MLB team logos or other licensed imagery
Minor league, international league, or non-MLB content
Commentary, news articles, scores from other sports
